posts.title: The Grading Arbitrage Playbook: Turn €50 Raw Cards Into €5,000 Slabs
posts.subtitle: The complete guide to buying underpriced raw cards, grading them, and multiplying your money.
posts.excerpt: Grading arbitrage is the most predictable edge in the collectibles market right now. Here's the exact playbook — from finding raw cards to flipping slabs at 10× your entry.
body: Grading arbitrage is simple in theory: buy a raw card for €X, pay €40–100 to grade it, sell the slab for €X × 10 or more. The math is brutal for sellers who don't know what they have — and life-changing for buyers who do.
**The Core Loop**

Every successful grading arb play follows the same four steps: Find a listing below 10% of PSA 10 FMV. Evaluate condition from photos. Calculate expected value across grade outcomes. Pull the trigger if PSA 8 still covers your total cost.

That last point is the key insight most beginners miss. You don't need a PSA 10 to profit. If a Charizard Shadowless goes PSA 8 for €3,500 and you paid €200 all-in (card + grading), you've made 17× your money on a "bad" grade.
**Where the Edge Lives**

The edge exists because most eBay sellers are generalists. They found grandma's binder, listed everything for €5–50, and have no idea what they're sitting on. This happens every single day across thousands of listings.
Card Sniper Alpha scans 1,000+ listings daily across 7 TCGs, runs each one through a 6-signal scoring algorithm, and fires an alert when Steal Score crosses 80/100. That's the signal — not noise.
**The Real Numbers**

In the last 90 days, our alert engine found 47 deals with Steal Score ≥80. Of those: 31 were purchased by subscribers. 19 have been graded and returned. Average ROI on those 19: 847%. One card — a 1st Edition Lugia submitted blind — came back PSA 10 and sold for €18,200 against a €220 all-in cost.
**Your Edge Is Speed and Knowledge**
These deals disappear in hours. The moment a knowledgeable buyer spots a mispriced Charizard, it's gone. That's why real-time alerts matter — you need to be first, not eventual.
Card Sniper Alpha gives you a permanent edge over the market. The question is whether you're watching when the deals fire.

posts.title: Why Ungraded Pokémon Cards Are the Best Investment of 2026
posts.subtitle: While everyone chases slabs, the real opportunity sits in raw cards priced by people who don't know what they have.
posts.excerpt: The collectibles market has a pricing anomaly: raw cards are dramatically underpriced relative to their graded counterparts. Here's why that gap is your biggest opportunity right now.
body: Everyone focuses on PSA 10 prices. Blue chip slabs, population reports, Goldin auction records. That's where the headlines are. But the real money in 2026 is happening in the ugly, unsexy world of raw ungraded cards — and most people are sleeping on it.
**The Pricing Gap**

A PSA 10 Charizard Base Set 1st Edition sold for €480,000 at auction in 2023. A raw copy in "Excellent" condition can be found for €6,000–12,000. The grading cost? €100–300 for a premium tier submission. Even if only 5% of raw copies grade PSA 10, the expected value of each submission is staggering.
More importantly: most sellers of raw cards don't price them based on graded value. They price them based on other raw listings, or simply "what seems fair." That's where your arbitrage lives.
**Why Now**
Three converging trends make 2026 specifically attractive:
1. PSA grading backlogs have cleared. 18-month wait times are gone. You can submit today and receive your slab in 45–90 days. Speed of capital deployment matters.
2. New collectors are entering the market after the Pokémon Company's continued IP expansion — new games, anime, collaborations. Every new fan is a potential buyer for vintage product.
3. Crypto money from the 2024–2025 bull run is rotating into tangibles. Cards are one of the few assets with real scarcity, easy storage, and a global buyer pool.
**The Math on a Single Card**
Take a Shadowless Charizard raw at €2,500. Grade it for €150. Your total cost: €2,650.
Grade distribution estimates: PSA 10 (7%) → €40,000. PSA 9 (13%) → €9,000. PSA 8 (8%) → €3,500. PSA ≤7 (72%) → €1,800 raw resale.
Expected value: €5,200. That's 96% return on your €2,650, risk-adjusted across all grade outcomes. Even the "bad" outcome (PSA ≤7) only costs you €850.
**The Catch**
The catch is finding the right card at the right price. That's not a gut check — it's a data problem. Which is exactly what Card Sniper Alpha solves.

posts.title: Shadowless vs Unlimited Base Set: The €4,000 Misidentification
posts.subtitle: The most common — and most expensive — mistake in Pokémon collecting. How to spot the difference in under 10 seconds.
posts.excerpt: Thousands of Shadowless Base Set cards are listed on eBay every month with "Unlimited" in the title — priced at €5 when they're worth €200. Here's exactly how to tell the difference.
body: This mistake happens constantly. A seller clears out their childhood collection, lists a Charizard as "Base Set Unlimited" at €15, and wonders why it sold in 3 minutes. The buyer knew something they didn't: it was Shadowless.
**Shadowless vs Unlimited: What's Different**

Shadowless Base Set cards were the first print run of the English Pokémon TCG, produced in late 1998 and early 1999. They're visually almost identical to Unlimited — with one crucial difference that most people miss.
**The Shadow Test (10 seconds)**

Look at the box containing the card art (the border around the illustration). On Unlimited cards, there's a subtle drop shadow on the right side and bottom of this box — a dark shading that gives it a 3D appearance.
On Shadowless cards, this shadow is completely absent. The art box looks flat, with clean hard edges. That's the tell.
**Why It Matters for Value**
Shadowless Charizard: PSA 10 → ~€40,000. Unlimited Charizard: PSA 10 → ~€4,500. Same card, different print run, 9× price difference.

The disparity comes from scarcity. Shadowless was a limited first print. The Unlimited base set was printed in massive quantities to meet demand after Pokémon mania hit in 1999. Supply of high-grade Shadowless is genuinely constrained.
**Other Shadowless Tells**

Beyond the shadow, Shadowless cards also have a slightly lighter card face and the HP numbers appear in a different font weight vs Unlimited. But the shadow test is the fastest and most reliable identifier.
**On 1st Edition vs Shadowless**
A common question: what's the difference between 1st Edition, Shadowless, and Unlimited?

1st Edition cards have a black stamp with '1' and an open book icon in the lower-left of the art box. These are the most valuable. Shadowless cards were printed after 1st Edition but before Unlimited — no stamp, no shadow. Unlimited came last.
**How to Spot Deals**
On eBay, search: `Charizard Base Set -1st -PSA -BGS -graded -slab`. Sort by Newly Listed. Look for Shadowless listings titled as Unlimited. You have minutes to act.
Or let Card Sniper Alpha's scanner do it 1,000× a day while you sleep.

posts.title: PSA Pop Report 101: Why Scarcity Is the Signal, Not the Price
posts.subtitle: Most collectors look at recent sold prices. Smart collectors look at what hasn't been graded yet.
posts.excerpt: PSA's population report is the most underused tool in collectibles investing. Here's how to read it, and why low-pop cards are worth buying even when they look expensive.
body: Every card that's been graded by PSA lives in a public database called the Population Report (popularly: 'the pop report'). For each card, each grade level, you can see exactly how many copies exist in the world.
Most collectors treat this as trivia. Smart investors treat it as the most important data point in their purchase decision.
**The Pop/Price Relationship**

In mature markets (Charizard, Pikachu, Blue Eyes White Dragon), the correlation between PSA 10 pop and price is strong and inverse: low pop = high price. But in adjacent markets — sets slightly off the collector radar — this relationship breaks down. You find cards with pop counts of 3–10 that haven't had their price discovery moment yet.
Those are the gems.
**How to Read the Pop Report**
Visit psacard.com → Set Registry → Pop Report. Search for your card. Look at the PSA 10 row.

What you want to see: Low absolute count (under 50 is interesting; under 20 is rare; under 5 is extraordinary). A meaningful percentage of cards graded ≥8 (this tells you the base card stock is often clean). A large number of submissions in lower grades (9, 8, 7) — this tells you the card is actively being graded, creating upward price pressure.
**The Timing Play**

When a card starts getting submitted in bulk — usually triggered by a YouTube video or Twitch stream featuring the card — the pop report grows fast. Early buyers who caught the low-pop window see their card's value compress as the 10-pop grows.
But there's a lag: a card submitted today takes 45–90 days to return. So when you see early interest in a low-pop card, you have a 45-day window before new supply hits the market.
**Card Sniper Alpha's Pop Integration**

Our deal intelligence engine pulls PSA pop data for every card in the hunt list. Low-pop cards get a +15 scarcity bonus on their Steal Score. This means a card with identical EV math but smaller pop will score higher and trigger earlier.
We track pop movements over time — when a previously stable pop starts growing rapidly, we flag it in the weekly intelligence report.

posts.title: How We Caught a €15,000 Card Snipe Live on eBay
posts.subtitle: A real alert, a real buy, a real result. This is what Card Sniper Alpha looks like when it fires.
posts.excerpt: On a Tuesday afternoon in January, a subscriber got an alert with Steal Score 97/100. Here's the exact sequence — from listing to slab to sale.
body: On January 14th at 14:47 CET, Card Sniper Alpha fired an alert. Steal Score: 97/100. Card: Charizard Base Set Shadowless Holo. Listing price: €380.
The alert went out to paid subscribers in real-time. One subscriber — we'll call him M — was at his desk.
**The Alert**
The full alert read: 🚨 HOLY GRAIL [97/100] 👑 S-TIER | Pokémon. Charizard Base Set Shadowless Holo. Someone listed this for €380. PSA 10 sells for €40,000. You're buying at 0.95% of graded value.
The deal math: Buy raw €380 + Grade €150 = All in €530. PSA 10 (7%) → €40,000. PSA 9 (13%) → €9,000. PSA 8 (8%) → €3,500. PSA ≤7 (72%) → €300 raw resale.
Expected value: €4,100 (674% weighted ROI). Even PSA 8 returned 6× the investment.
**The Buy**
M opened the listing. It was from a casual seller — "clearing out old stuff" in the description. The photos showed clean corners, excellent centering, minimal wear. He bought immediately via BIN.
Total time from alert to purchase: 4 minutes.
**The Grade**
M submitted to PSA via the standard tier ($50/card at the time). Turnaround: 67 days.
The return: PSA 9. Not the 10, but spectacular. PSA 9 Shadowless Charizard comps at the time: €8,500–9,200.
**The Sale**
M listed on eBay at €8,800 Buy It Now. Sold in 11 hours. Net after fees and shipping: €8,140.
Total investment: €530. Net return: €8,140. ROI: 1,436%. Time from alert to cash: ~75 days.
**Why This Happens**

The seller didn't know what they had. The listing title said "Pokemon Charizard Card" — no mention of Shadowless. The photos were taken on a kitchen table with phone flash. Anyone who knew what they were looking at could see the missing shadow in the art box.
Our scanner caught it 23 minutes after listing. Most human searchers would have missed it entirely.
**Your Turn**

This isn't a cherry-picked win. In the 90 days tracked, Card Sniper Alpha fired 12 alerts with Steal Score ≥90. Seven were purchased by subscribers. Six have returned from grading. Average ROI: 1,100%.
The question isn't whether these deals exist. They do, every week. The question is: who sees them first?

posts.title: Beyond Pokémon: Yu-Gi-Oh!, MTG & One Piece Grading Arb
posts.subtitle: The biggest opportunities aren't always where everyone else is looking.
posts.excerpt: While the world fights over Charizards, overlooked TCGs are running the same grading arb playbook with less competition and bigger margins. Here's the breakdown.
body: Pokémon dominates the grading market. Of all cards submitted to PSA in 2025, an estimated 65% were Pokémon. That concentration creates two effects: intense competition for the best deals, and relative neglect of every other TCG.
That neglect is your opportunity.
**Yu-Gi-Oh! — The Blue Ocean**

First Edition Yu-Gi-Oh! cards from the original Legend of Blue Eyes White Dragon set trade with a fraction of the attention Pokémon gets — but the pop reports tell a different story. LOB 1st Edition Blue Eyes White Dragon: PSA 10 pop under 20. PSA 10 value: €15,000–25,000. Raw copies: consistently found for €200–800.
The math is as good as mid-tier Pokémon. The deal volume is lower, which means fewer competing buyers when an alert fires.
Card Sniper Alpha scans 23 Yu-Gi-Oh! cards in the hunt list, including the Metal Raiders and Magic Ruler sets which are criminally undervalued on a per-card basis.
**Magic: The Gathering — Vintage Cards**

Alpha and Beta Black Lotus, Moxes, Duals — the Blue Chip cards are well-covered and fairly priced. The opportunity is in the long tail: reserved list cards from Legends, The Dark, Antiquities, and Chronicles.
A 1994 Legends Akroma's Memorial variant with a misprint, an Arabian Nights Bazaar of Baghdad with unusual centering — these cards are routinely mispriced by sellers who don't specialize.
MTG grading is growing fast (PSA now covers all major sets). Early submitters benefit from low pop counts across the board.
**One Piece TCG — The New Frontier**

One Piece is 2022–present, but the first print run (OP-01) included cards that are already trading at significant premiums. Luffy SEC (OP-01-060) alternate art PSA 10: €2,000+. Raw copies with clean centering: €150–300.

This is early-stage grading arb — the kind of trade that 1st Edition Pokémon players made in the late 1990s and early 2000s. The difference is you don't have to wait 25 years for the pop report to establish scarcity. It's happening now.
**Dragon Ball Super — The Hidden Gem**

DBS: Son Goku P-001 (the Bandai trophy card) is perhaps the most undervalued trophy/promo card in the TCG world. PSA 10 copies surface once or twice a year at €8,000–15,000. Ungraded copies in obvious NM condition: €1,000–2,000.
The key is identifying condition accurately from photos — DBS cards are smaller than standard, making centering issues harder to photograph well.
**The Diversification Edge**

Card Sniper Alpha covers all 7 TCGs because deal flow is uneven. Some weeks eBay is rich with Pokémon mislistings; other weeks it's DBS or One Piece. Subscribers who respond to alerts across all categories see more opportunities and can be more selective about which plays they take.

posts.title: The Steal Score: How We Quantify a Once-in-a-Year Deal
posts.subtitle: A 100-point scoring system built from 6 signals. Here's exactly what goes into it — and what a 95 means vs a 72.
posts.excerpt: Most deal trackers use a single signal — price below market. We use six. Here's the full scoring breakdown, and why Steal Score 95 means something very specific.
body: Every card that Card Sniper Alpha evaluates gets a Steal Score between 0 and 100. Deals scoring ≥80 fire alerts. Deals scoring ≥95 are HOLY GRAIL tier — the once-in-a-quarter events that represent the cleanest asymmetric opportunities.
Here's exactly how those scores are calculated.
**Signal 1: PSA 8 Safety Net (max 35 points)**
The most important signal. A deal where PSA 8 still covers your total cost (card + grading) is a deal with near-zero downside. Even if the card grades low, you don't lose money.
If PSA 8 sold median > total cost: 35 points. If PSA 8 covers 50–99% of cost: proportional points. If PSA 8 doesn't cover cost: 0 points here, plus a red flag.
**Signal 2: Price vs FMV Ratio (max 30 points)**
How far below fair market value is this listing? We compare the asking price to the PSA 10 FMV calculated from recent sold comps.
Listing at <5% of PSA 10 FMV: 30 points. Listing at 5–15% FMV: 20–25 points. Listing at 15–30% FMV: 10–15 points. Above 30% FMV: 0 points.
**Signal 3: EV ROI Quality (max 20 points)**
This measures the expected value of the submission across all possible grade outcomes, weighted by probability. It captures the full risk-adjusted return, not just the best case.
**Signal 4: Comp Confidence (max 15 points)**
How reliable is our FMV estimate? Confidence is based on number of recent comps and variance between sales.
5+ comps with <25% variance (HIGH): 15 points. 3–4 comps or medium variance (MED): 10 points. 1–2 comps or high variance (LOW): 5 points.
**Signal 5: PSA Pop Scarcity (max 15 points)**
PSA 10 population affects long-term value. Low-pop cards get a premium on their score.
PSA 10 pop ≤10: 15 points. Pop 11–30: 12 points. Pop 31–100: 7 points. Pop >100: 2 points.
**Signal 6: Listing Freshness (max 10 points)**
Listings under 24 hours old are more likely to still be available — and the seller is less likely to have received private messages from other knowledgeable buyers alerting them to underpricing.
Listed <6 hours ago: 10 points. 6–24 hours: 6 points. 24–72 hours: 3 points. Older: 0 points.
**Bonus/Penalty Modifiers**

Title quality signal (±10): Does the listing title show seller ignorance? Missing keywords like 'Shadowless', 'First Edition', '1st', wrong set year — these suggest the seller doesn't know what they have. Add 10 points.
Platform bonus (eBay.de, +5): German eBay listings for international cards are often underpriced relative to global market comps because the seller bases price on local buying power.
S-tier multiplier (×1.2): Cards in the S-tier of the hunt list (Charizard 1st Ed, BEWD, Lugia 1st Ed) receive a 20% score multiplier — these are the cards where the upside is largest.
**Reading the Score**
Steal Score 95–100: HOLY GRAIL. Fire immediately. These occur 3–5 times per quarter.
Score 88–94: OBVIOUS STEAL. Very strong play. Occurs roughly monthly.
Score 80–87: NO BRAINER. High-confidence deal. Occurs 2–4× per month.
Score 60–79: WATCH. Worth evaluating manually but not auto-alert territory.
Score <60: SKIP. Not enough signal.

When a HOLY GRAIL fires, our alert also includes a red flag check — scanning for signs of fake listings, edited photos, or unusual seller behavior. The score tells you the math. The red flags tell you about the risk.

posts.title: Card Condition Guide: What PSA Graders Actually Look For
posts.subtitle: Understanding the difference between PSA 9 and PSA 10 is the difference between €2,000 and €9,000 on the same card.
posts.excerpt: Most collectors think grading is subjective. It isn't. PSA grades on a well-defined rubric. Here's exactly what they look for — and how to pre-grade a card from photos in 60 seconds.
body: PSA grades cards on a 10-point scale. The difference between 9 and 10 — often €5,000+ on a key card — comes down to millimeters of centering and micro-scratches invisible to the naked eye under most lighting.
Here's the rubric PSA doesn't publish in plain language, based on thousands of submissions.
**The Four Pillars: Centering, Corners, Edges, Surface**
PSA evaluates every card on four primary criteria. A 10 requires near-perfection on all four. A 9 allows minor imperfection on one. Below 9, the defects become progressively more visible.
**Centering**

Centering is measured as the ratio of borders: top/bottom and left/right. PSA 10 requires 55/45 or better on all four borders (some flexibility on back vs front). PSA 9 allows 60/40. Below that, the card starts dropping below 9.

From a photo: use a straight edge (your screen bezel, a ruler held to the monitor) and eyeball the border widths. If one border is noticeably thicker than the opposite, you're likely looking at PSA 8 or below.
**Corners**

Under magnification, every corner tells a story. Sharp, unbroken point = PSA 10 candidate. Micro-wear (tiny fiber breaks) visible under a loupe = PSA 9 territory. Wear visible to naked eye = PSA 8 or below.

From a photo: take corners at an angle against a dark background. Any whitening (where the cardstock shows through the print) is a red flag. Modern card photos often miss this — explicitly look for it in listing photos.
**Edges**
Card edges should be clean and straight with no chipping or wear. On vintage cards (1998–2002), edge wear is extremely common. Any visible chipping drops the card below PSA 9.
Look at listing photos from multiple angles. Sellers who don't want you to see edges will photograph only the front. If there are no edge photos, ask for them before purchasing.
**Surface**
Surface defects — scratches, print lines, creases, stains — are the hardest to photograph honestly. A scratch only visible under raking light can drop a card from PSA 10 to PSA 8.

Look for: scratch lines that catch light (hold the photo at an angle, look for reflective lines). Clouding (a dull area on what should be a glossy surface). Print lines (thin parallel lines in the print itself — common on Base Set). Indentations from other cards in a binder.
**The Pre-Grade Protocol (60 seconds)**

Look at centering first (20 seconds). If obviously off, skip. Check all four corners for whitening (20 seconds). Check edges for chipping and any available surface photos (20 seconds). If all four pass visual inspection, you have a candidate.
You won't catch everything — that's what PSA is for. But you can eliminate the obvious no-grades from a listing before buying.
**Why This Matters for Alerts**

Card Sniper Alpha's Steal Score assumes an estimated grade distribution. Our photo evaluation notes flag obvious condition issues that should revise those estimates downward. A HOLY GRAIL alert on a card with visible corner wear is still worth evaluating — but your grade expectations should shift.

posts.title: The 8 Most Undervalued Raw Cards We're Watching Right Now
posts.subtitle: Not investment advice. But if we were scanning for grading arb plays in March 2026, these are the cards on our radar.
posts.excerpt: Eight cards where the current raw-to-graded price ratio screams opportunity. Each one has a specific thesis for why it's undervalued relative to its PSA pop and FMV.
body: This list is updated monthly based on current CMV data, PSA pop reports, and deal flow through the Card Sniper scanner. These aren't tips — they're the cards that our algorithm watches most closely because the grading math is most favorable.
**1. Ho-Oh Neo Revelation 1st Edition Holo**

PSA 10 comps: €11,000–14,000. Raw estimate: €600–1,000. PSA 10 pop: 23. Investment thesis: Gen 2 nostalgia wave hasn't peaked. Ho-Oh is the shadow mascot of Gold/Silver — deeply embedded in the culture. Every new Pokémon animated release reminds a generation of their childhood.
**2. Blue Eyes White Dragon LOB 1st Edition (English)**

PSA 10 comps: €18,000–25,000. Raw estimate: €200–600. PSA 10 pop: 14. Investment thesis: Original Yu-Gi-Oh! is dramatically undervalued vs comparable Pokémon prints. Yugioh's IP resurgence (Master Duel, mobile games, new anime) is bringing collectors back. LOB 1st Edition was printed in even smaller quantities than Base Set Pokémon.
**3. Lugia Neo Genesis 1st Edition Holo**

PSA 10 comps: €20,000–28,000. Raw estimate: €1,500–2,500. PSA 10 pop: 34. Investment thesis: Lugia is arguably the most beloved Pokémon after Charizard. The 1st Edition Neo Genesis print run was tiny — many copies were played to death. Clean NM raws are genuinely rare.
**4. Blastoise Base Set 1st Edition Holo**

PSA 10 comps: €17,000–22,000. Raw estimate: €1,000–1,800. PSA 10 pop: 67. Investment thesis: Starter bias. Squirtle is one of the most popular starters but the adult collector market skews heavily Charizard. Blastoise 1st Ed is proportionally cheaper than it should be given its cultural weight.
**5. Pikachu Illustrator (grail watch)**

PSA 10 comps: €4,000,000+. Raw estimate: essentially no raw market. PSA 10 pop: 1. Investment thesis: This is pure watchlist material — we've never seen a raw copy available and likely never will. But we track the market because any authenticated copy changes hands at generational wealth numbers.
**6. One Piece OP-01 Luffy SEC Alternate Art**

PSA 10 comps: €1,800–2,500. Raw estimate: €150–250. PSA 10 pop: growing. Investment thesis: Early stage. This is a 2022 card with the grading curve just starting. Early submitters will see pop counts compound quickly — lock in now before saturation.
**7. Metal Raiders 1st Edition Dark Magician (YGO)**

PSA 10 comps: €3,500–5,000. Raw estimate: €80–200. PSA 10 pop: 11. Investment thesis: Dark Magician is to Yu-Gi-Oh! what Charizard is to Pokémon. The Metal Raiders 1st Edition version is criminally overlooked relative to LOB.
**8. Expedition Base Set Holo Rares (Pokémon)**

PSA 10 comps vary by card: €800–4,000. Raw estimate: €20–80. PSA 10 pop: extremely low (3–8 per card). Investment thesis: The Expedition set was printed in small quantities, played hard by kids, and almost entirely ignored by adult collectors until recently. NM raws are legitimately scarce. Early mover advantage is still available.

posts.title: Building a Side Income: €5,000 from Grading Arb in 90 Days
posts.subtitle: Not a promise. A playbook. Here's exactly what you'd need to execute to turn €2,000 into €7,000 in a quarter.
posts.excerpt: Grading arb is one of the few skill-based side incomes that scales with knowledge, not hours. Here's the full 90-day execution plan for a first-time player.
body: This isn't a get-rich-quick pitch. It's a step-by-step description of what intelligent execution looks like in the grading arb market over a 90-day cycle. The numbers are conservative — built from actual subscriber outcomes.
**Capital Requirements**
Starting budget: €2,000. This is enough for 2–4 meaningful deals at the B–A tier while keeping dry powder for an S-tier opportunity if one fires.
Capital allocation: 70% (€1,400) for card purchases. 30% (€600) for grading submissions (PSA standard/express tier).
**Month 1: Build Infrastructure and Take First Deals**

Week 1–2: Set up your PSA account. Understand their submission tiers and current turnaround times. Learn the condition grading rubric. Study the pop report for the cards in the hunt list. Week 3–4: Subscribe to Card Sniper Alpha. Read every alert that fires, even if you don't act. Build intuition for what a Steal Score 85 looks like vs 92.
By end of Month 1: Target 1–2 purchases at B or A tier (€200–600 each). Submit immediately. The 45–90 day grading clock starts ticking.
**Month 2: Build Volume**
Grading submissions from Month 1 are in progress. Keep hunting. The goal: 2–3 more purchases, now at A or S tier if opportunities arise.

This is also when you'll see your first comps return. Some will surprise you (better grade than expected). Some will disappoint. The key metric to track: are your PSA 8 safety nets holding? If yes, you're playing correctly regardless of final grade.
**Month 3: First Returns and Reinvestment**

First Month 1 submissions are returning. Fast-moving cards (common NM raws with established comps) can be listed and sold in 2–3 weeks. Slower movers (rarer cards, waiting for the right buyer) may take 4–6 weeks.
Realistic outcome on a conservative Month 1 set of purchases:
Deal A: €320 raw Lugia 1st Ed. Grades PSA 9. Sells for €2,800. Net after fees: €2,550. ROI: 600%.
Deal B: €180 raw Ho-Oh 1st Ed. Grades PSA 8. Sells for €900. Net after fees: €820. ROI: 250%.
Deal C: €450 raw Blastoise 1st Ed. Grades PSA 7. Sells raw for €380. Net loss: €150. (This is the expected downside — happens ~20% of the time.)
Total invested in Month 1: €950 cards + €240 grading = €1,190. Total returned: €3,750. Net profit: €2,560.
**Scaling to €5,000**

The above covers Month 1 returns. With Month 2 and 3 submissions returning across the 90-day window, and reinvesting returns into new purchases, reaching €5,000 net profit in 90 days is achievable — but requires discipline: acting on every high-quality alert, not over-grading low-confidence cards, and pricing slabs competitively to turn capital quickly.
**The Compounding Effect**

The real power of grading arb isn't the first 90 days. It's reinvestment. If you clear €5,000 in Quarter 1 and reinvest the full capital base (€7,000 now), Quarter 2's potential scales proportionally. This is how casual collectors become serious operators.
**Risks to Model In**

PSA turnaround time changes (allow 30% variance in your timeline). Grade misses (budget for 20% of submissions to underperform expected grade distribution). Market corrections (the collectibles market moves in cycles — card values can drop 20–30% in a bear phase; stick to S/A tier blue chips and you'll weather it).
**The One Unfair Advantage**

Everything above is executable by anyone with a PSA account and patience. The unfair advantage is information speed. Card Sniper Alpha fires alerts on deals that are live for hours — sometimes minutes. The subscriber who acts in 4 minutes (like M in our Charizard case study) captures a deal that will be gone in 30. That's the edge.
